1: Psalm 133, attributed to David, is one of the Songs of Ascents and one of the most concentrated poems in the Psalter — three verses that explore the beauty of covenant brotherhood. "How wonderfully good (mah-tov umah-naim) it is, how delightful, when brothers (ahim) live together as one (yashev yahdav)!" — the exclamation of aesthetic pleasure in communal covenant unity. Tov (good) and naim (pleasant, delightful, sweet) are wisdom-aesthetic judgments: this is not merely a moral evaluation but a claim about the beauty of covenant community life. "Brothers living together as one" — yahdav (together, as a unity): the kinship language of "brothers" encompasses the covenant community that is constituted as a family by its shared relationship to the one God. The pilgrimage festivals created exactly this — the scattered tribal communities gathered together in one place, living as brothers for the duration of the feast.
2: "It is like costly anointing oil poured on the head (rosh), running down over the beard — Aaron's beard — flowing all the way down to the collar of his robes (pi middotav)" — the anointing oil of the high priest: the sacred anointing oil (shemen hammishha) of Exodus 30:22-33 — myrrh, cinnamon, cassia, and olive oil — was used exclusively for consecrating the high priest and the sanctuary furnishings (Exodus 30:26-32). Its application to Aaron (Leviticus 8:12: "Moses poured some of the anointing oil on Aaron's head and anointed him") was the act of priestly consecration, the setting apart of the person for divine service. The oil flowing generously from head to beard to collar represents the full, excessive anointing — nothing held back. The image for brotherhood: the unity of brothers at Zion is like being caught up in the excess of consecrating oil, like being part of the priestly community where the Spirit's presence flows without measure.
3: "It is like the dew of Hermon (tal-hermon) descending on the mountains of Zion — for there the LORD has commanded the blessing: life to the age (hayyim ad-haolam)" — Mount Hermon (2,814m, the highest peak in the region) collects moisture from the Mediterranean that falls as extraordinarily heavy dew; it is one of the wettest points in the entire Levant. The image of Hermon's dew descending on Zion's mountains (much lower, much drier) is deliberately abundant and generous — it would require a different weather system from what actually occurs, but the hyperbole makes the theological point: the blessing of fraternal unity at Zion is like receiving Hermon-level abundance in a Zion-level environment. "There the LORD has commanded the blessing" — the divine command (tzivvah) is what causes the blessing to flow; it is not a natural phenomenon but a covenant act. "Life to the age (hayyim ad-haolam)" — the eschatological telos: fraternal unity at Zion is the foretaste of the eternal life that belongs to the coming age, the life that the covenant community will know fully when the divine dwelling is complete (Revelation 21:22-22:5).
